coredata.dc:title: Amplified electrochemiluminescence detection of CEA based on magnetic Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles-assembled Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites combined with multiple cycling amplification strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Jie, Guifen
Ge, Junjun
Gao, Xiaoshan
Li, Chunli
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we designed a new strategy for ultrasensitive detection of CEA based on efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) quenching of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped SiO2 nanocomposite by ferrocene using target recycling amplification technique. A large number of Ru@SiO2 ECL signal probe were firstly assembled on the novel magnetic core−shell Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles (NPs), then the ferrocene-labeled ECL quenching probe (Fc-probe) was linked to the magnetic NPs. Finally, numerous DNA1 sequences were produced by target CEA-triggered multiple recycling amplification and displaced the Fc-probe on the magnetic NPs, leading to significantly enhanced ECL signal for CEA detection. Because of the designed cascade signal amplification strategy, the newly developed method achieved a wide linear range of 10 fg/mL to 10 ng/mL with a ﻿low detection limit of ﻿ 3.5 fg/mL. Furthermore, taking advantages of the magnetic Fe3O4@Au NPs for carring abundant signal probes, sensing target and ECL detection, the developed ECL strategy is convenient, rapid and displayed high sensitivity for CEA detection, which has great potential for analyzing the clinical samples in practical disease diagnosis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Ru@SiO2
Target recycling amplification
Fe3O4@Au
Quenching probe

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carboxyl functionalized Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites
Feasibility of the strategy for CEA detection based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites
Principle of CEA detection based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites and cyclic amplification technology
PAGE analysis of the target recycling amplification strategy
Characterization of the modified electrodes at different stages
ECL detection of CEA based on Ru@SiO2 probe and target recycling amplification strategy
Selectivity of the ECL strategy for CEA detection
Application of the ECL strategy in real samples for CEA detection
Comparison of the ECL strategy with other CEA detection methods

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575072 ), and the Key Research and Development Plan of Shandong Province ( 2016GGX102021 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Ru@SiO2, (B) UV−vis absorption spectra of Ru@SiO2, Ru@SiO2-cDNA, SiO2, (C) FL spectra of Ru@SiO2, Ru@SiO2-cDNA, SiO2.
(A) ECL–potential curve of Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au on the electrode, (B) ECL signals during nine cycles under continuous CV scans. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set at − 700 V.

ECL signals of the electrode at different stages. (a) The bare electrode, (b) Fe3O4@Au modified electrode, (c) Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au modified electrode, (d) Fc/Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au modified electrode, (e) Fc/Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au modified electrode in the presence of target thrombin (10 ng/mL).

PAGE analysis of the target recycling amplification strategy: (a) HP-DNA, (b) HP-DNA+Target, (c) HP-DNA+Target+Primer, (d) HP-DNA+Target+Primer+phi29+dNTPs +Nb.BbvCI, (e) HP-DNA+Primer+phi29+dNTPs+Nb.BbvCI.

(A) ECL signal responses to different concentrations of CEA: (Ι0) 0 fg/mL, (a) 10 fg/mL, (b) 0.1 pg/mL, (c) 1.0 pg/mL, (d) 10 pg/mL, (e) 0.1 ng/mL, (f) 1.0 ng/mL, (g) 10 ng/mL; (B) The logarithmic calibration curve for detection of CEA in the range of 10 fg/mL to 10 ng/mL.
Schematic representation for ECL detection of CEA based on (A) target recycling amplification strategy, and (B) Ru@SiO2 probe.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different approaches for CEA detection.

head.ce:title: Amplified electrochemiluminescence detection of CEA based on magnetic Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles-assembled Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites combined with multiple cycling amplification strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we designed a new strategy for ultrasensitive detection of CEA based on efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) quenching of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped SiO2 nanocomposite by ferrocene using target recycling amplification technique. A large number of Ru@SiO2 ECL signal probe were firstly assembled on the novel magnetic core−shell Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles (NPs), then the ferrocene-labeled ECL quenching probe (Fc-probe) was linked to the magnetic NPs. Finally, numerous DNA1 sequences were produced by target CEA-triggered multiple recycling amplification and displaced the Fc-probe on the magnetic NPs, leading to significantly enhanced ECL signal for CEA detection. Because of the designed cascade signal amplification strategy, the newly developed method achieved a wide linear range of 10 fg/mL to 10 ng/mL with a ﻿low detection limit of ﻿ 3.5 fg/mL. Furthermore, taking advantages of the magnetic Fe3O4@Au NPs for carring abundant signal probes, sensing target and ECL detection, the developed ECL strategy is convenient, rapid and displayed high sensitivity for CEA detection, which has great potential for analyzing the clinical samples in practical disease diagnosis applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) analysis has attracted considerable attention due to its high sensitivity, easy controllability, low background, low cost, and has been widely employed in food safety (Cao et al., 2013; Xie et al., 2016) clinical diagnosis, pharmaceutical analysis (Richter, 2004; Yang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2015) and environmental monitoring (Cheng et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2015). Luminol (Dong et al., 2014), quantum dots (Li et al., 2015), Ru(bpy)3 2+ and complexes as the major ECL luminophores have high ECL performance, good electrochemical stability and controllability, be called "artificial ECL probe" (Feng et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2015; Santra et al., 2001). For ultrasensitively sensing trace amounts of target molecules, many efforts have been made to enhance the emitted light intensity of ECL sensors. Up to now, signal amplification strategies have been widely used that mainly involved nanomaterial-based amplification (Chen et al., 2017a), enzymatic signal amplification (Chen et al., 2017b; Li et al., 2012), and nucleic acid amplification (Zhang et al., 2017; Ge et al., 2017).

At present, with the rapid development of nano materials, nanomaterial-based amplification has become the preferred signal amplification strategy to improve the sensitivity of a probe because of the large specific surface area, strong electronic transmission capability, and convenient labeling of nanomaterials (Lei and Ju, 2012). Among them, SiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) have attracted much attention due to their special pore structure, high specific surface area and good biocompatibility. Tan's research group prepared a Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped SiO2 NPs (Ru@SiO2) and acted as a fluorescent probe for detection of leukemia cells (Bae et al., 2010). Since then, the SiO2 NPs with good structure have been immobilized with a large number of Ru (bpy)3 2+ and used as amplified ECL signal probe to achieve ultrasensitive analysis of nucleic acids (Li et al., 2014), proteins (Chen et al., 2013), and cell (Ricci-Vitiani et al., 2007), which greatly improved the detection sensitivity.

In recent years, researchers have been working on more feasible DNA amplification strategies which have played an important role in ECL analysis, such as rolling circle amplification (RCA) (Hong et al., 2016; Hsieh et al., 2014) loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) (Wu et al., 2015), strand displacement amplification (SDA), hybridization chain reaction (HCR) (Guo et al., 2016; Zang et al., 2015), catalytic hairpin assembly technology (CHA) (Chen et al., 2015). In particular, SDA is a nicking endonuclease-assisted isothermal polymerization reaction activated by specific primer and creating many products of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA), which has been widely used in ECL, fluorescence and electrochemical analysis for its high efficiency and easy operation (Yin et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014; Frens, 1973).

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is one of the most common biomarkers, they are frequently associated with malignancies such as colon cancer, gastric cancer, pancreatic cancer, and breast cancer (Munge et al., 2016). Serum CEA levels in healthy volunteers are 3–5 ng/mL, sometimes 10 ng/mL in some benign diseases (Laxman et al., 2008). Therefore, it is necessary to detect CEA biomarker exactly in tumor prevention and early clinical diagnosis (Toh et al., 2015). In recent years, researchers have been trying to find a new way for rapid, accurate and highly sensitive detection of CEA. So far, a variety of biological analysis methods, such as enzyme-linked immunoassay (Zhao et al., 2015), fluorescence (Wang et al., 2015a), electrochemical method (Lv et al., 2015), ECL (Tu et al., 2012), photoelectric assays (Zhu et al., 2016) have been reported. In addition, some novel nano-composite materials such as two-dimensional graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheets (Chen et al., 2014) and hydrazide-modified graphene QDs (Dong et al., 2015) were applied to develop novel ECL immunosensors for CEA detection.

In this work, a novel Ru(bpy)3 2+ doped SiO2 (Ru@SiO2) nano-composite material with intense ECL was prepared, and applied to develop a new ECL biosensor for sensitive detection of CEA based on effective ECL quenching of Ru@SiO2 by Fc and multiple cyclic amplification technology. The Ru@SiO2 ECL probe was assembled on the magnetic core-shell nanoparticles (Fe3O4@Au), and then the ECL signal was quenched by hybridizing with Fc-DNA. In the presence of target CEA, a large amount of DNA1 was produced by target recycling amplification strategy and replaced Fc-DNA, the ECL signal is enhanced and used for detection of CEA. This ECL biosensor shows excellent performance for detecting tumor marker in practical applications.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment. Sulfhydryl beads (3–3 diameter µm) was obtained from BaseLine ChromTech Research Centre (Tianjin，China). Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O was purchased from bailingwei Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing,China). Phi29 polymerase, Nb.BbvCI endonuclease, and dNTPs were purchased from New Zealand Biotechnology (Beijing) Co., Ltd. Triton X-100, cyclohexane, hexyl alcohol, Tetraethyl Orthosilicate (TEOS), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was purchased from Shanghai lingchao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All the DNA used in this experiment were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The corresponding sequences are summarized below ( Table 1)﻿.

CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China). MPI-A electrochemiluminescence analyzer (Xi'an Remex Analyse Instrument CO., LTD) with three-electrode system. The reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, the working electrode was a gold electrode (5 mm), and a Pt wire was the counter electrode. JEM-2000EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and JSM-6700F field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Japan). Lambda 35 UV Visible Spectrometer (Japan), Fluorescence Spectrometer F-4600.

Gold nanoparticles were prepared according to the methods reported in the previous literature (Frens, 1973). Briefly, 100 mL of ultra-pure water was added to the flask and heated to boiling. Then 2.0 mL of 1% HAuCl4 solution was added to the water under vigorous stirring and kept boiling for 10 s. Subsequently, 3.8 mL of 2 wt% of sodium citrate solution was added to the flask, and the boiling lasted for 1.0 h. The color of the solution became red, indicating the formation of monodisperse spherical particles. Then the heating source was removed, and the solution was cooled to room temperature by magnetic stirring.

200 µL of magnetic beads were washed three times with PBS buffer (pH 7.4), then 1.0 mL of gold NPs was added, and incubated at 37 °C in constant temperature oscillator for 16 h. Excess gold nanoparticles were removed by magnetic separation, then Fe3O4@Au was dispersed in 200 µL of PBS and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carboxyl functionalized Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The carboxyl functionalized Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites were prepared according to the methods reported in the previous literature (Wang et al., 2015b). First, 5.31 mL Triton X-100, 22.5 mL of cyclohexane, 5.4 mL of n-hexyl alcoho and 1.02 mL of water were added to the three-necked flask respectively to form a water in oil microemulsion. Then, 240 µL of 0.1 M Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution and 300 µL of TEOS were added to the emulsion. Polymerization was initiated by adding 180 µL of NH4OH. After 24 h of reaction, Ru@SiO2 was purified by acetone and separated by centrifugation. The surfactant molecules and unreacted Ru were removed for many times with ethanol and water, and yellow Ru@SiO2 nanoparticles were obtained.

Then the precipitation was dispersed in the ethanol solution, then 500 µL of vinyltriethoxysilane (VTES) was added to the solution, reacting for 12 h under continuous stirring. Next, the nanoparticles were washed and then dispersed in the mixed solutions of 0.1 mg/mL KMnO4 and 4.2 mg/mL NaIO4, reacting for 6 h to obtain the carboxyl functionalized Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites.

1.0 mL of Ru@SiO2 was placed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 10 min, then the precipitation was re-dispersed in 1.0 mL of Tris-HCl solution (0.1 M, pH=7.4), and activated with the solution containing 30 µL of EDC (0.1 M) and 30 µL of NHS (0.025 M) for 30 min at room temperature. After adding 50 µL of cDNA (10 µM) to the activated Ru@SiO2, the mixture was incubated at 37 ℃ for 5 h with gentle shaking. The resultant mixture was separated by centrifugation, washed with ultrapure water, and redispersed in 0.1 M Tris-HCl (pH 7.4).

200 µL of Ru@SiO2-cDNA probe was added to 100 µL of Au@MBs solution, and reacted for 12 h with gentle shaking. After magnetic separation, the resultant mixture was washed to remove the unbound Ru@SiO2-cDNA probe. Finally, the Ru@SiO2-cDNA-Fe3O4@Au solution was processed with 50 µL of 1.0 μM mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) for 2 h to block the nonspecific binding sites. Then the product was magnetically separated, washed, and re-dispersed in Tris-HCl solution.

Prior to use, HP-DNA was treated by heating at 95 ℃ for 3 min, then cooled down to room temperature to form the hairpin structure. The reaction process was as follows: A reaction system containing 10 µL of 1.0 µM HP-DNA, 10 µL of 1.0 µM Primer, 20 µL of 300 µM dNTPs, 5 U phi29 DNA polymerase, and 5 U Nb.BbvCI endonuclease were prepared, then 5 µL of CEA target solution at different concentrations (10 ng/mL to 10 fg/mL) was added to the solution. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C in the constant temperature oscillator for 80 min to obtain DNA1.

Preparation of ECL probe solution: 20 µL of Ru@SiO2-cDNA-Fe3O4@Au was added to the centrifuge tube, then 20 µL of 1.0 µM Fc-DNA was added to the solution, reacting in the constant temperature oscillator for 2 h. After that, the product is magnetically separated, washed and re-dispersed in the Tris-HCl solution. Subsequently, the prepared DNA1 was added to the solution, and reacted at 37 °C for 2 h, then it was separated, washed, and re-dispersed in the ultrapure water.

The gold electrodes were polished with 0.3 and 0.05 µm alumina powder separately, and rinsed with ultrapure water. Then, the electrodes were ultrasonically treated in 50% HNO3 and 50% ethanol solution for 3 min; Next, the electrodes were further electrochemically cleaned in 0.5 M H2SO4 with potential scanning from − 0.3 and 1.5 V until a reproducible cyclic voltammogram was obtained, followed by rinsing with ultrapure water and drying with nitrogen. Subsequently, the electrodes were immersed in the prepared ECL probe solution, then the MBs with the signal probe were assembled on the electrode by magnetic force adsorption. The modified electrodes were rinsed with Tris-HCl, ECL detection was carried out in a PBS buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M TPA. The scanning potential is from 0 to 1.2 V, the scanning rate is 0.1 V/s, and the photomultiplier tube is − 700 V.

Fig. 1(A) showed the TEM image of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites, the morphology are basically spherical, and the particle size is about 50 ± 5 nm. Fig. 1(B) showed the UV−Vis absorption spectra of the water-soluble Ru@SiO2, Ru@SiO2-cDNA, and SiO2 nanocomposites. Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites exhibit two absorption peaks at 288 nm and 454 nm, respectively (Lou et al., 2015). The peak at 454 nm is due to the charge transfer of metal to ligand in Ru(bpy)3 2+, whereas the absorption peak at 288 nm is a transition between the internal ligands (π → π*). After conjugation of cDNA to Ru@SiO2, a slight shift of the peak at 288 nm was observed.

Fig. 1(C) showed the fluorescence (FL) spectra of Ru@SiO2, Ru@SiO2-cDNA and SiO2. (Excitation wavelength: 275 nm). Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites and Ru@SiO2-cDNA showed fluorescence peak at 599 nm, and the intensity is very high, indicating that the prepared Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites have good optical properties. Compared with Ru@SiO2, the peak position at 599 nm of Ru@SiO2-cDNA has not changed, but the intensity decreased, which may be due to the decrease of concentration after conjugation of cDNA to Ru@SiO2, or the effects of oligonucleotide on the fluorescence intensity of Ru@SiO2.

Fig. S1 showed the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the SH-Fe3O4 (A) and Fe3O4@Au (B), it can be seen that the size of the thiol-modified magnetic Fe3O4 bead is uniform and the surface is smooth, the particle diameter is about 4 µm. After the AuNPs were assembled on the bead, a layer of very small particles were observed, and the surface of the bead became rough and rugged, indicating that the AuNPs has been successfully modified on the surface of the bead.

The Ru@SiO2 assembled on the Fe3O4@Au displayed very high ECL signal ( Fig. 2A), indicating that the prepared nanocomposite has good ECL properties, and can be applied to the ECL biosensing. On the ECL-potential curve, a strong ECL peak at + 1.16 V was observed, which is due to the reaction of Ru (bpy)3 2+ with the coreactant TPA. Ru(bpy)3 2+ modified on the electrode was oxidized to Ru(bpy)3 3+, and TPA was oxidized to produce cation radical, then a proton was lost to generate the free radical TPA•. Ru(bpy)3 3+ reacted with TPA to generate the excited state Ru (bpy)3 2+*and emitted ECL. The reaction mechanism is as follows:
(1) Ru(bpy)3 2+－e- → Ru(bpy)3 3+
(2) TPA－e- → TPA•+
(3) TPA•+ → TPA• +H+
(4) Ru(bpy)3 3+ + TPA• → Ru(bpy)3 2+* + TPA+
(5) Ru(bpy)3 2+* → Ru(bpy)3 2+ + hν

Fig. 2(B) showed the ECL signals under continuous cyclic voltammetry scan for nine cycles. It can be observed that the ECL signals are high and stable, suggesting that the Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au is suitable for ECL detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the strategy for CEA detection based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility for CEA detection based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposite was investigated. Fig. 3 showed the responses of the ECL signal at different stages. The bare electrode has no ECL performance (curve a). When the Fe3O4@Au were magnetically adsorbed on the electrode (curve b), the ECL signal was very low. The reason may be that a little impurity in the solution could generate excited state, and the Fe3O4@Au accelerated the electron transfer reaction, which could emit a little ECL. When the Ru@SiO2 was linked to the Fe3O4@Au by cDNA, very high ECL response (curve C) was generated, indicating that the synthesized Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites have strong ECL properties. When the quencher Fc was linked to the Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Fe3O4@Au, the ECL signal decreased significantly (curve d), indicating that Fc could effectively quench the ECL signal of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites. When the target CEA, DNA probe, phi29 polymerase, and Nb.BbvCI endonuclease were present simultaneously, the ECL response signal improved significantly (curve e), indicating that the strategy based on ECL quenching of Ru@SiO2 combined with enzyme-aided cycle amplification technology could be applied to the target CEA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of CEA detection based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposites and cyclic amplification technology
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, a novel Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped SiO2 nanocomposite was prepared, and used as ECL signal probe for sensitive detection of CEA based on efficient ECL quenching by ferrocene combined with target recycling amplification strategy. Scheme 1 consists of two parts: A (target recycling amplification process) and B (ECL detection process). In part A, the HP-DNA consists of three parts: primer complementary DNA sequence a (blue), DNA sequence c for specific recognition site of Nb.BbvCI (pink), and the aptamer for CEA (red). In the presence of target CEA, it can specifically bind to its aptamer and led to the conformation change of HP-DNA, so the blue DNA terminal sequence for primer recognition was exposed. With the action of phi29 DNA polymerase, the primer extended along the blue DNA template to release CEA, resulting in the strand displacement amplification reaction (cycle I). At the same time, the formed complementary strands of HP-DNA were cleaved by Nb.BbvCI and released the black DNA1 d, which further resulted in the cyclic polymerization and cleaving process (cycle II). After many cycles, a lot of DNA1 was released.

In part B, the carboxyl functionalized Ru@SiO2 nanoparticles as signal probe were firstly linked to the cDNA with amino groups. Then, they were immobilized on the core−shell Fe3O4@Au magnetic beads (MBs) by Au-S bond, followed by conjugating with the ferrocene-labeled probe (Fc-probe) for ECL quenching through DNA hybridization. Numerous DNA1 sequences were produced by above target recycling amplification process and displaced the Fc-probe on the magnetic bead, leading to the ECL enhancement of the signal probe. The changes of the ECL signal is quantitatively related to the concentration of target CEA, which could be used for CEA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAGE analysis of the target recycling amplification strategy
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the target recycling amplification strategy was investigated by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) analysis. As shown in Fig. 4, lane a represented the HP-DNA, exhibiting a distinct single band. In the presence of target CEA, it can specifically bind to its aptamer and led to the conformation change of HP-DNA, so a new electrophoresis dark band appeared (Lane b), which indicated the formation of CEA@HP-DNA complexes. Then the added primer hybridized with the exposed DNA sequence of HP-DNA, as the base numbers of primer is fewer, the hybridization of primer with HP-DNA has little effect on the PAGE results, so lanes b and c showed similar bands. Upon the addition of phi29 DNA polymerase, dNTPs, and Nt.AlwI, DNA polymerization and subsequent Nt.AlWI-aided cleavage reactions occurred, lane d showed the PAGE results for the produced DNA strands. The upper deep colored electrophoresis band is the CEA@HP-DNA@primer complex, and the new narrow band is the cleavage DNA sequences. Without target CEA, HP-DNA could not opened, a series of subsequent polymerization and cleavage reactions could not carried out, therefore lanes e and a have the same bands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the modified electrodes at different stages
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S2 showed the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, A) and cyclic voltammetry (CVs, B) for the modified electrodes at different stages. By comparison, with the increasing assembly of Fe3O4@Au, cDNA, Ru@SiO2, MCH, Fc-DNA, and DNA1, the electron transfer resistance (Ret) gradually increased (curve a to g). Similarly, the assembly process of the modified electrodes were further characterized by CVs, the corresponding peak current gradually decreased with increasing modification, the results were in agreement with EIS. Therefore, both the EIS and CVs results indicate that the DNA1/Fc-DNA/MCH/Ru@SiO2/cDNA/Au@MB modified electrode was successfully fabricated for ECL detection.

In order to achieve the best performance of detection experiments, the experimental conditions were optimized. Fig. S3(A) showed the ECL signal responses to different reaction time for combining SH-DNA to Fe3O4@Au with 10 ng/mL target CEA. It can be seen that the ECL signals increased gradually with increasing reaction time, and then reached a plateau after 12 h, indicating that 12 h is the best time for the experiment.

Fig. S3(B) showed the ECL signals to different time for strand displacement amplification (SDA) reaction with 10 pg/mL target CEA. With the extension of reaction time, the ECL signal gradually increased, and did not change after 80 min due to the consumption of primer, so 80 min was chosen in the experiment.

Fig. S3(C) showed the ECL signal responses to different concentrations of phi29 polymerase and Nb.BbvCI endonuclease with 10 pg/mL target CEA. The ECL signals gradually increased with the dose of enzyme and became stable at 5 U of enzyme, indicating that 5 U of phi29 polymerase and endonuclease were optimal for the experiment.

Fig. S3(D) showed effect of dNTPs concentration on ECL responses in the strand displacement amplification (SDA) reaction with 10 pg/mL target CEA. With increasing concentration of dNTPs, the reaction products increased, so the ECL signal gradually increased and reached the maximum at 300 µM, thus 300 µM dNTPs was the best value for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of CEA based on Ru@SiO2 probe and target recycling amplification strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5A showed the ECL signal responses corresponding to different concentrations of target CEA. When the target CEA was absent, ECL of Ru@SiO2 probe was quenched by Fc, a lower background signal was observed (Curve I0). In the presence of target CEA, as the CEA concentration increased, the ECL signals increased gradually (a to g), indicating that the concentration of the target CEA could be detected by the change of ECL signal.

Fig. 5B showed the standard calibration curve for detection of CEA. The value of ΔIECL is linearly related to the logarithm of target CEA concentration (R = 0.9967) in the range of 10 fg/mL to 10 ng/mL. According to the standard dilution approach for the calculation of the LOD (Guo et al., 2013), the detection limit is 3.5 fg/mL. In order to evaluate the precision of this method, a series of five repeated measurements of 10 ng/mL target CEA were carried out, the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) was 7.8%, indicating that the method had good reproducibility in detecting CEA. According to this linear equation, the concentration of target CEA can be quantitatively determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the ECL strategy for CEA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the ECL strategy was investigated by using several proteins, such as AFP, BSA, p24 and thrombin, as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. S4, when the target CEA was absent, the background signal and the ECL responses to the interfering proteins are lower. The ECL signal responses are higher only in the presence of the target CEA. The results suggest that the ECL signal responses were achieved only by combining the aptamer with the target CEA, so the ECL strategy has good selectivity for target detection in biological analysis and provides a good method for early diagnosis of cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the ECL strategy in real samples for CEA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential application of the strategy based on ECL of Ru@SiO2 in real sample analysis, the experiment of the ECL strategy was conducted by detecting CEA in human serum samples. The human serum sample obtained from the hospital (Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, China) was utilized to test the feasibility. The samples were diluted 103-fold with Tris-HCl buffer, and the concentration was 6.56 pg/mL. The average measured concentration of the diluted CEA sample by this work is 6.50 pg/mL, which is consistent with the results obtained from the hospital, indicating that the present strategy can be potentially applied to real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the ECL strategy with other CEA detection methods
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed ECL strategy with recent CEA detection methods were compared ( Table 2), the results demonstrated that our work for CEA detection have wider linear range, higher sensitivity and lower detection limit, which are comparable to those reported ones.

A novel Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped SiO2 nanocomposite was prepared and used as ECL signal probe for sensitive detection of CEA based on efficient ECL quenching by ferrocene combined with target recycling amplification strategy. The magnetic Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles were acted as nanocarrier to immobilize large number of Ru@SiO2 signal probe, which not only displayed amplified ECL signal but also are convenient for separation, assembly and detection process. Moreover, multiple target recycle amplification strategy was used to further amplify ECL signal of Ru@SiO2 nanocomposite, which greatly improved the detection sensitivity. The developed ECL strategy displayed high sensitivity, good selectivity, wide linear range and excellent performance for practical application, which has great potential in biomedical and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575072), and the Key Research and Development Plan of Shandong Province (2016GGX102021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.07.046.
